segments.text: Welcome back!
It's June 24, 2026, for your daily robotics digest.
We've got major M&A news in the Boston Dynamics space, Agility going public, and a look at ASML powering the next chip revolution.
Tune in for all eight updates.
From Humanoids Daily, "Hyundai to Take Full Ownership of Boston Dynamics in $325 Million SoftBank Buyout."
The report details an agreement wherein Hyundai is set to gain complete ownership of Boston Dynamics through a substantial buyout from SoftBank.
This acquisition will result in Boston Dynamics becoming a wholly owned subsidiary under the corporate umbrella of Hyundai Motor Group.
Following the completion of this purchase, the company has indicated plans to move toward a rapid listing on the Nasdaq exchange, with an Initial Public Offering targeted for 2028.
The development marks a significant consolidation within the robotics sector, indicating increased major corporate investment in advanced humanoid technology going forward.
From Humanoids Daily, "Agility to Go Public in $2.5 Billion SPAC Merger with Churchill Capital XI."
This report details Agility's plan to achieve a public listing through the use of a special purpose acquisition company merger.
The company is utilizing a vehicle associated with dealmaker Michael Klein to execute this transaction.
The proposed merger valuation stands at two point five billion dollars, facilitating the listing on public exchanges.
Industry sources indicate that Agility intends to capitalize on increasing consumer interest in humanoid robotics technology.
This positioning marks the firm as the first Western pure-play entity within the burgeoning humanoid sector.
This development signals a significant capital influx for the physical robotics domain and warrants close observation from institutional investors tracking automation trends.
From technologyreview.com, "ASML's Giant Machine Powers Future Microchip Revolution."
The report details how specialized lithography machines are becoming crucial for developing advanced microprocessors.
These complex instruments are necessary to pattern the incredibly small circuits onto silicon wafers that power modern electronics.

To achieve higher density and capability in chips used across smartphones and artificial intelligence servers, ASML utilizes extreme-ultraviolet or EUV light technology, achieving a resolution of eight nanometers on its newest models.

The development of this machinery represented a decade-long research effort costing ten billion dollars, resulting in units priced at approximately four hundred million dollars each due to sustained industrial demand for capability enhancement.
This technological barrier reduction will continue to dictate the pace of advancement within next-generation computing hardware.
From The Robot Report, "Deep dive into ARM’s physical AI and robotics strategies with Drew Henry."
This report details advancements in developing artificial intelligence systems intended for deployment within real-world operational constraints.
Specific discussion areas included the progress being made in autonomous systems capable of maneuvering through complex, uncontrolled environments.
The methodology discussed centers on bridging the significant gap between sophisticated artificial intelligence models and tangible electromechanical hardware.
Furthermore, the segment emphasized the crucial focus on creating reliable AI architectures suitable for applications operating outside strictly controlled laboratory settings.
These strategies point toward a maturing integration point between advanced computation and physical robotics.
Industry observers anticipate increased practical deployment of robust, outdoor-ready autonomous machines in the near future.
From The Robot Report, "Why physical AI 2.0 needs a reality check."
The piece argues that achieving true advancement in physical artificial intelligence hinges significantly upon developing robust physical state recovery capabilities.
This technical ability is posited as the key enabler for robots to achieve meaningful and effective interaction with their operational environments.
The analysis suggests that concentrating solely on advancements in vision processing or natural language understanding proves insufficient for next-generation hardware deployments.
Therefore, incorporating an accurate, real-time knowledge base regarding the robot's own physical condition is deemed necessary for tangible progress across the field.
This focus directs industry development toward integrated systems management moving forward.
From The Robot Report, "Vention collaborates with FANUC and Universal Robots on software-defined automation."
This news details a multi-party partnership designed to simplify the integration of advanced robotics within manufacturing settings.
The collaboration involves Vention partnering with established industry leaders like FANUC and Universal Robots.

The core methodology centers on developing "software-defined automation," which aims to streamline the entire process for manufacturers designing and subsequently deploying industrial and collaborative robot systems.
This joint effort is intended to broaden access to complex robotic capabilities across a wider spectrum of manufacturing clientele.
The announcement indicates a notable trend toward software standardization in robotics, suggesting increased usability for smaller production facilities.
From The Robot Report, "ExRobotics launches UL-certified inspection robot for hazardous environments."
The report details the release of a new piece of equipment engineered for high-risk operational areas.
ExRobotics introduced the ExR-2.5, an advanced inspection robot specifically designed to navigate and survey dangerous settings.
A key aspect of this launch is that the model has attained UL certification, which verifies its safe deployment within explosive atmospheres throughout North America.
The technology incorporates specialized safety protocols allowing for operation in environments previously deemed too risky for standard inspection equipment.
This development signals a marked advancement in industrial automation capabilities for sectors requiring rigorous safety compliance.
From MIT Technology Review - AI & Emerging Tech, "This flying solar-powered platform could deliver better internet from the air."
The report details a large aerial system designed to boost internet connectivity across significant geographic areas.
Developed by Sceye in New Mexico, this massive, silver, solar-powered aircraft is scheduled for a deployment beginning in August.
The platform has dimensions of approximately 200 feet in length and is slated to traverse the southwestern United States before crossing the Pacific Ocean en route to Japan.
Upon arrival near the Japanese coast, the system intends to maintain an altitude of about 18 kilometers above sea level for its operational duration.
This initiative suggests a tangible development pathway for providing high-capacity communications links via airborne infrastructure.
And that’s your robotics roundup for June 24th, 2026!
Looks like even legged bots need a friendly parent.
Thanks for tuning into the circuit flow—keep building, and we'll catch you tomorrow!
